title: Hazel wattle fence panel
subtitle: A traditional woven hazel panel for garden screening and windbreak
excerpt: A wattle fence panel from coppice hazel: 180 cm long, 90 cm tall, the horizontal rods woven through vertical stakes. The traditional method for making a portable garden screen or windbreak in a day. Coppice hazel is the ideal material; flexible enough to weave when green, rigid enough when dried to hold the panel shape without a frame.
sourceNotes: Traditional British wattle hurdle and fence panel construction; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. I for standard panel dimensions and stake spacing.

content.text: A hazel
wattle

panel is the simplest form of basketry scaled up to garden use: vertical stakes set into a working frame, horizontal rods woven through in alternating passes. One person can make a 180 by 90 cm panel in a day from freshly cut
coppice
hazel. The finished panel is lightweight, can be carried flat, and lasts 8 to 12 years in garden use before the stakes decay at the base.
The wood

Stakes: 9 straight hazel or ash rods, 110 cm long and 4 to 5 cm diameter, pointed at one end. Weavers: 30 to 40 hazel rods, 2 to 2.5 m long and 1 to 2 cm diameter; the long flexible shoots from the same coppice stool. Fresh green hazel is best; if using partially dried hazel, soak the rods for 2 hours before weaving. All material should be cut from a managed coppice stool and de-branched before work starts.
Tools

A Yorkshire billhook for harvesting and de-branching. A bodkin for opening the weave at each pass-through. A sloyd knife for pointing the stake ends and trimming weaver ends. A leather strop. A working frame; two parallel beams 180 cm apart on the ground with 9 holes drilled at 20 cm spacing; is optional but speeds up staking.
Method
Point all 9
stakes
and drive them into the working frame or into the ground at 20 cm spacing. The stakes should project 100 cm above the ground level. Begin weaving from the bottom.

Work the first rod through the stakes alternately: in front of stake 1, behind stake 2, in front of stake 3, and so on. Push the rod down to the base as tight as possible. Work the second rod the opposite way: behind stake 1, in front of stake 2, behind stake 3. Each rod locks the previous one in place.

Always start new rods on the same side (left or right) to maintain a consistent weave pattern. The thick butt end of each rod goes in at the start side, the thin tip finishes at the other. This keeps the thickness consistent across each row.

Use the bodkin to open a passage for each weaver at the point where it crosses a stake; push the bodkin through between the weaver and the stake to create a gap, then thread the new rod through. This keeps the weave tight without forcing rods past each other.

As the panel height builds, press each completed row firmly downward with the heel of the hand before starting the next. The weave should be dense enough that a rod cannot be easily pulled free by hand.

Continue weaving until the panel reaches 90 cm. Trim the rod ends flush with the outer stakes with the billhook. The top row should end with rods tucked firmly behind the outer stake on each side; a loose top row unravels under wind load.
Finishing

Leave the panel to dry in place for 2 weeks before moving. The hazel will shrink slightly as it dries, tightening the weave. Once dry, the panel can be moved and installed permanently. Drive the stakes 25 cm into the final installation ground to anchor the panel.
Variations
Willow weavers: thinner, more flexible rods for a finer weave. The same construction method applies; reduce stake spacing to 15 cm.
Hurdle frame with mortise slots: an older build. The top and bottom rails sit in slots cut into the stakes. You can skip the working frame. The result is a self-contained portable panel.
Care

Replace individual stakes when they show decay at the base; work the weaving rods loose around the failed stake, extract it, and drive a new pointed stake into the same position before re-threading. The weavers often last twice as long as the stakes.

attrs.intro: Common problems with hazel wattle panels.

items.fix: Soak dry rods for 2 hours before use. Use the bodkin to open a passage at each stake rather than pushing the rod through by force.
items.cause: Hazel too dry or too cold, or rods forced past stakes rather than woven through

items.fix: Re-drive the outer stakes. If the panel has been in place for more than 2 weeks, a horizontal brace rod lashed to the back of the panel at mid-height will correct the bow.
items.cause: Top and bottom rows not compressed firmly enough, or outer stakes not driven deep enough
items.symptom: Panel bows outward in the centre after installation

items.fix: After each rod, press the full length of the row down with both palms before starting the next. The row should feel firmly locked before proceeding.
items.cause: Inconsistent downward pressure applied to each row before the next is started
items.symptom: Weave is uneven; some rows tight, some loose

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
